entries.when_to_use: When melting butter (or similarly delicate dairy fats with milk solids) on a stovetop, where scorching/browning is a failure mode.
entries.strategy: Use a saucepan (direct-heat vessel, not a mixing bowl) on low heat. Unwrap the butter first so wrappers don't melt/burn. Swirl or nudge with a spoon every ~20 seconds to keep contact even and prevent the bottom from scorching while the top is still solid. Pull the pan off the burner when only a few small lumps remain and let residual heat finish melting — this avoids browning the milk solids (which starts above ~120 C).

entries.when_to_use: When choosing a vessel for a cooking task that requires applying heat from a stove burner.
entries.strategy: Select a vessel rated for direct heat (saucepan, skillet, pot) rather than a mixing bowl or other prep-only container. Match the vessel to the burner size and place it flat and centered before adding ingredients.

entries.when_to_use: When a cooking process has a narrow window between 'done' and 'ruined' (e.g., melting, toasting, tempering, reducing).
entries.strategy: Stop active heating slightly before the target endpoint and exploit residual heat (carry-over) from the hot vessel to finish the job. Move the pan off the burner onto a cool trivet rather than relying on turning the knob off alone, since burners and pans retain heat.

entries.when_to_use: When tasked with bringing a pot of water to a boil on a stovetop.
entries.strategy: Place the pot on a burner first, cover with a lid to trap heat and steam, then turn the burner to HIGH. Stay nearby and watch for the progression from small bottom bubbles to a vigorous rolling boil (typically several minutes); do not leave the stove unattended.

entries.when_to_use: When heating liquids or cooking tasks where reaching temperature faster is beneficial and no reason exists to leave the vessel uncovered.
entries.strategy: Cover the vessel with a lid before applying heat — trapping steam and heat significantly reduces time-to-boil and conserves energy.

entries.when_to_use: When sequencing physical setup steps that involve energy sources (stoves, ovens, heaters).
entries.strategy: Stage all passive components (place pot on burner, add lid, position items) BEFORE activating the energy source. This avoids handling hot equipment and ensures energy transfers to the intended target from the moment it turns on.

entries.when_to_use: Frying a sunny-side-up egg on a stovetop with a non-stick pan and butter available.
entries.strategy: Preheat the pan on medium-low before adding fat; add ~1 tsp butter and wait until it melts and lightly foams (but does not brown) as a temperature indicator. Crack the egg close to the pan surface to keep the yolk intact, then leave undisturbed for 2–3 minutes until whites are fully opaque while the yolk stays domed and runny. Cut the heat before sliding onto a plate so residual heat doesn't overcook the yolk.

entries.when_to_use: Any stovetop cooking task where pan temperature must be judged without a thermometer.
entries.strategy: Use butter (or a similar fat) as a visual thermometer: a thin, lightly foaming, golden layer that does not brown indicates correct medium-low heat. Browning butter means the pan is too hot; unmelted or sluggish butter means it's too cool — adjust the burner before adding the main ingredient.

entries.when_to_use: Cooking delicate proteins (eggs, fish, custards) where carryover heat can overcook the dish after plating.
entries.strategy: Turn the burner off slightly before the food looks fully done and transfer it off the hot pan promptly. Residual heat from the pan and the food itself will finish cooking it, preserving texture (e.g., a runny yolk or moist fish center).

entries.when_to_use: When tasked with freezing a liquid (e.g., water) into solid form in an environment containing both a refrigerator and a freezer.
entries.strategy: Choose the freezer, not the refrigerator — only the freezer reaches sub-zero temperatures needed for solidification. The fridge merely chills.

entries.when_to_use: When freezing water and a shallow, compartmented container (like an ice tray) is available alongside the original cup.
entries.strategy: Transfer the water into the ice tray before freezing. The larger surface area and thinner depth give better thermal contact and faster, more even freezing than leaving it in a deeper cup.

entries.when_to_use: When placing a liquid-filled container into a freezer or any moving/storage step.
entries.strategy: Set the container flat and level on the shelf to prevent spills and ensure an even frozen surface, then close the door promptly to preserve the cold environment.

entries.when_to_use: When a task requires a physical state change (e.g., freezing, baking, drying) that takes real time to complete.
entries.strategy: Budget enough wait time for the process to finish before checking — for freezing a standard tray of water, allow roughly 2–4 hours in a typical freezer. Don't open the door prematurely, as that disrupts the cold environment.

entries.when_to_use: When combining dry and wet ingredients (e.g., flour, salt, water) into a uniform mixture or dough.
entries.strategy: Mix dry ingredients together first (flour + salt) until evenly distributed, then add the liquid gradually in small increments while stirring. This prevents salt pockets and avoids over-hydration. Finish by kneading to develop a smooth, uniform texture.

entries.when_to_use: When the environment offers tools or appliances (e.g., stove, freezer) that are not required by the stated goal.
entries.strategy: Do not use tools just because they are available. Re-read the goal and only invoke heating, chilling, or other transformations if they are explicitly needed. For pure 'combine' or 'mix' goals, mechanical mixing alone is sufficient.

entries.when_to_use: When a task involves achieving a target consistency or uniformity (e.g., dough texture, sauce smoothness) rather than a discrete state change.
entries.strategy: Work incrementally and observe after each step: add small amounts, stir/knead, then reassess before adding more. Stop when the observed texture matches the goal description, rather than following a fixed recipe quantity.

entries.when_to_use: When melting chocolate (or other heat-sensitive ingredients that scorch easily above ~115°F) for dipping or coating, and a stove, saucepan, and heat-safe bowl are available.
entries.strategy: Use a double-boiler setup: add ~2 inches of water to a saucepan, set burner to low, break the chocolate into small even pieces in a heat-safe bowl, then rest the bowl on the saucepan rim so steam (not direct flame) melts it without the bowl touching the water. Stir slowly with a spatula to distribute heat and prevent hot spots. As soon as the chocolate is fully liquefied and glossy, turn off the burner and lift the bowl onto a dry towel to halt cooking before scorching or seizing.

entries.when_to_use: Before applying heat to a solid food item (chocolate bar, butter stick, block of cheese, etc.) that needs to melt uniformly.
entries.strategy: Unwrap and break/cut the item into small, evenly sized pieces before heating. Smaller, uniform pieces melt faster and more evenly, reducing the chance that some portions scorch while others remain solid.

entries.when_to_use: When cooking or melting a delicate ingredient where overheating causes irreversible damage (scorching, seizing, curdling, breaking).
entries.strategy: Default to the gentlest viable heat source (low burner, indirect steam, residual heat) and remove from heat immediately once the target state is reached — don't leave it on the stove 'just to be sure.' Transfer to a dry, room-temperature surface like a towel to arrest further cooking.

entries.when_to_use: When tasked with heating a liquid (soup, water, sauce) on a stovetop to a boil or simmer.
entries.strategy: Place the pot on a burner first, cover with a lid left slightly ajar to trap heat while letting steam escape, then turn the burner to High. Stir occasionally with a utensil to distribute heat and prevent scorching on the bottom. Maintain High heat until the surface shows large rolling bubbles for a vigorous boil (or reduce earlier for a simmer).

entries.when_to_use: When the goal specifies a 'vigorous' or 'rolling' boil rather than just heating or simmering.
entries.strategy: Do not lower heat once small bubbles or steam first appear — a simmer is not a vigorous boil. Keep the burner on High and wait the extra minute or two until the entire surface erupts in large, churning bubbles before declaring success.

entries.when_to_use: When covering a pot that is being heated on a stove.
entries.strategy: Set the lid loosely with a small gap rather than sealing it tightly. This traps most of the heat to speed boiling while preventing dangerous pressure buildup and boil-over.

entries.when_to_use: When heating thick or chunky liquids (soups, stews, sauces with solids) on high heat.
entries.strategy: Stir periodically with a wooden spoon or similar utensil to redistribute heat and lift solids off the bottom; otherwise vegetables or starches can stick and scorch before the liquid reaches a boil.

entries.when_to_use: After achieving a vigorous boil when the task goal has been met.
entries.strategy: Confirm the goal state (rolling bubbles across the whole surface) before stopping. Leaving a covered pot at a rolling boil risks boil-over, so plan to reduce heat shortly after — but do not reduce heat before confirming the goal is achieved.

entries.when_to_use: Cooking rice on a stovetop using the absorption method with a pot, lid, water, and dry rice.
entries.strategy: Use a 2:1 water-to-rice ratio. Combine rice and water in the pot, bring to a rolling boil on high heat uncovered, then cover and reduce to low for about 18 minutes without lifting the lid. Turn off heat and let rest covered for 5 minutes, then fluff with a fork to separate grains.

entries.when_to_use: Any simmer-and-absorb cooking step where a covered pot needs to finish via residual heat.
entries.strategy: After the active simmer time, turn off the burner and let the pot rest covered for ~5 minutes before uncovering. This lets trapped steam finish absorption evenly and prevents gummy or unevenly cooked results from premature lid removal.

entries.when_to_use: Bringing liquids to a boil then transitioning to a controlled simmer on a stove.
entries.strategy: Start uncovered on high heat to reach a rolling boil quickly, then immediately cover and drop the burner to low to settle into a gentle simmer. Avoid lifting the lid during the simmer phase, since each lift releases steam and disrupts the absorption/temperature balance.

entries.when_to_use: When cooling a carbonated beverage (soda, sparkling water, beer) from warm to cold-to-the-touch in a kitchen with both fridge and freezer available.
entries.strategy: Choose the refrigerator over the freezer. Freezing a carbonated, sealed bottle risks bursting from expansion and CO2 pressure. Place the bottle upright on a shelf with room, close the door to retain cold air, and wait ~2–3 hours for steady heat transfer before checking by touch.

entries.when_to_use: When an action requires waiting for a slow physical process (heat transfer, cooling, freezing, drying) to reach a target tactile/visual state.
entries.strategy: Don't check immediately — estimate a realistic duration based on the process (e.g., ~2.5 hours for a warm bottle to chill in a ~4°C fridge), wait that full interval, then verify by the relevant sense (touch, sight) before declaring success.

entries.when_to_use: When placing an object inside an appliance or container with a door (fridge, freezer, oven, microwave).
entries.strategy: Treat it as a three-step sequence: open the door, place the item stably (upright, on a shelf with clearance, not blocking airflow), then close the door to restore the internal environment. Forgetting to close the door defeats the appliance's function.

entries.when_to_use: When multiple tools/appliances could accomplish a goal but differ in risk to the object being acted on.
entries.strategy: Pick the option that preserves object integrity even if it's slower. Faster, more aggressive options (freezer vs fridge, high heat vs low heat) often introduce failure modes (rupture, cracking, scorching) that destroy the goal. Bias toward the gentler tool when the goal allows the extra time.

entries.when_to_use: When combining raw eggs with a liquid (e.g., milk) to make a uniform omelette or scrambled-egg base.
entries.strategy: Crack eggs one at a time on the bowl's rim to avoid shell fragments, add roughly 1 tablespoon of milk per egg, then whisk briskly in a circular motion for ~30 seconds until the mixture is smooth, pale-yellow, and slightly frothy with no streaks of clear white or unmixed milk.

entries.when_to_use: After whisking a batter or egg mixture and before declaring it done.
entries.strategy: Scrape the whisk (or a spatula) along the sides and bottom of the bowl and give a few final stirs to incorporate any unmixed pockets clinging to the walls, ensuring full uniformity.

entries.when_to_use: When melting a block of hard cheese (e.g., cheddar) for a sauce on a stovetop.
entries.strategy: Grate the cheese first to maximize surface area, warm a liquid medium (e.g., ~1 cup milk) in the saucepan on low heat, then add the shredded cheese in handfuls while whisking continuously until smooth and glossy. Remove from heat promptly to avoid breaking the emulsion.

entries.when_to_use: When heating protein-rich dairy (cheese, cream sauces) that can seize, curdle, or turn grainy.
entries.strategy: Use low-and-slow heat with constant agitation (whisking), and pull the pan off the burner as soon as the target texture is reached rather than holding it over heat.

entries.when_to_use: When a solid ingredient risks scorching if placed directly on a hot dry pan.
entries.strategy: Introduce a liquid medium into the pan first and let it come up to temperature before adding the solid, so the solid melts/cooks into the liquid rather than contacting bare metal.

entries.when_to_use: Heating scorch-prone liquids like milk or cream on a stove, especially when the target is a gentle simmer rather than a boil.
entries.strategy: Start on medium-low (never high), stir continuously with a flat-edged utensil while scraping the bottom to prevent solids from settling and scorching, and avoid leaving the pot unattended.

entries.when_to_use: Targeting a specific temperature band on a stove (e.g., a bare simmer at 180-185°F / 82-85°C for milk) where overshooting causes failure (scalding, boilover).
entries.strategy: Clip a thermometer to the side of the pot with the tip submerged but not touching the bottom, and proactively reduce heat well before the target (around 170°F for an 180-185°F goal) since temperature continues to rise after a heat reduction.

entries.when_to_use: Confirming a steady-state condition has been reached (e.g., a simmer holding, a process stabilized) before declaring success and moving on.
entries.strategy: After hitting the target, hold at the reduced setting for a short dwell (e.g., ~30 seconds) while observing that the indicator stays stable, then remove the driving input (turn off heat) rather than declaring success on first touch of the target.

entries.when_to_use: Searing meat (especially steak) in a pan and aiming for a browned crust rather than a steamed gray exterior.
entries.strategy: Pat the meat thoroughly dry and salt it ~15–20 min ahead so surface moisture evaporates and the meat tempers off fridge-cold. Surface water must boil off before Maillard browning can start, so any visible moisture sabotages the crust.

entries.when_to_use: Using a cast-iron (or heavy) pan to sear protein on a stovetop.
entries.strategy: Preheat the pan DRY on high for ~3–4 minutes until it just begins to smoke; confirm readiness with the Leidenfrost test (water beads skitter). Add a thin film of high-smoke-point oil only right before the protein goes in — adding oil too early burns it and adding it to a cool pan causes sticking and steaming.

entries.when_to_use: Protein is in contact with a hot searing surface and a crust is forming.
entries.strategy: Do not move or flip the protein until a deep crust has set (~2–3 min per side for a ~1-inch steak). A properly seared side will release cleanly from the pan on its own; if it sticks, it is not ready to flip. Fidgeting interrupts Maillard development.

entries.when_to_use: Cooking a steak to a target doneness (e.g., medium-rare) using an instant-read thermometer.
entries.strategy: Pull the steak ~5°F (3°C) below the target final temp to account for carryover cooking during rest. For medium-rare, pull at ~125°F (52°C) so it coasts to ~130–131°F. Probe the thickest part, not near bone or fat.

entries.when_to_use: Steak (or similar muscle cut) just came off the heat and you are about to slice it.
entries.strategy: Rest the meat loosely tented on a board for roughly 5–10 minutes (about half the cook time) before slicing, then slice against the grain. Cutting immediately spills juices onto the board and leaves the interior wet-gray instead of evenly pink.

entries.when_to_use: When solidifying a warm liquid or semi-liquid mixture (e.g., ganache, custard, gelatin) into a firm, scoopable consistency in a kitchen with refrigerator and freezer available.
entries.strategy: Transfer the warm mixture into a shallow dish to maximize surface area for even cooling, then chill in the refrigerator (not the freezer, which causes uneven hardening and condensation) on a level shelf. Leave undisturbed for ~2 hours, then verify readiness by gently pressing the surface — it should feel firm but yield slightly, indicating a pliable, scoopable texture.

entries.when_to_use: When combining oil and vinegar (or any oil + water-based liquid) into a stable vinaigrette or similar emulsion in a bowl with a whisk.
entries.strategy: Use roughly a 3:1 oil-to-vinegar ratio. Pour the vinegar into the bowl first and begin whisking it briskly to create an agitated base, then drizzle the oil in a thin, steady stream while whisking continuously — never dump the oil in all at once. Continue whisking for ~15 seconds after all oil is added, then pause to confirm the mixture stays cloudy and uniform rather than separating into layers.

entry.when_to_use: When whisking eggs (alone or with milk/dairy) into a uniform liquid base, e.g., for omelettes, scrambles, or custards.
entry.strategy: Crack eggs cleanly on the bowl's rim one at a time (discarding shells), add a small amount of dairy (~1 tbsp milk per egg), then whisk briskly in a circular motion until the yolks break and the mixture becomes uniformly pale-yellow with no streaks. Finish by scraping the bowl's sides and bottom to incorporate any clinging egg white.

entry.when_to_use: When creating an emulsified dressing or sauce from immiscible liquids like oil and vinegar (vinaigrette).
entry.strategy: Use roughly a 3:1 oil-to-vinegar ratio. Pour the vinegar (the smaller, water-based phase) into the bowl first and begin whisking it briskly to agitate it. Then drizzle the oil in a thin, steady stream while whisking continuously — never dump oil in at once. After incorporation, whisk vigorously for ~15 more seconds and pause to confirm the emulsion holds without immediate separation.

entries.when_to_use: Melting a solid fat (butter, lard, ghee) on a stovetop where the goal is liquid fat without browning or scorching.
entries.strategy: Use a saucepan (direct-heat-safe vessel, not a mixing bowl) on low heat. Unwrap the fat first so wrappers don't melt/burn. Swirl or nudge every ~20 seconds so the bottom stays in contact with the pan but doesn't scorch while the top is still solid. Pull off the heat when only a few small lumps remain and let residual heat finish melting — this avoids browning the milk solids.

entries.when_to_use: Melting a scorch-sensitive ingredient (chocolate, delicate sauces, custards) where direct stovetop heat would burn or seize it.
entries.strategy: Build a double boiler: put ~2 inches of water in a saucepan, set it on LOW heat until it produces steam, then rest a heat-safe bowl on the saucepan's rim so the bowl is warmed by steam — not touching the water and not over direct flame. Break the ingredient into small, even pieces first for uniform melting. Stir slowly with a spatula to distribute heat and prevent hot spots (chocolate seizes from overheating or stray water). Pull the bowl off the saucepan and kill the burner as soon as the mass is fully smooth and glossy — residual heat will keep melting any remaining bits and further cooking risks scorching or seizing.

entries.when_to_use: Choosing between similar containers (e.g., saucepan vs. mixing bowl, pot vs. bowl, cup vs. ice tray, deep bowl vs. shallow dish) for a cooking, heating, cooling, or freezing task.
entries.strategy: Match the vessel to the thermal process. For stovetop heat: only use direct-heat-rated cookware (saucepan, pot, skillet) on the burner, placed on the burner BEFORE turning on the heat. For high-heat searing specifically: prefer heavy cast-iron or carbon steel over thin nonstick — they hold heat through the thermal shock of a cold steak hitting the surface and tolerate dry preheats to smoke point. For indirect/gentle heat: a heat-safe bowl set over a steaming saucepan (double boiler) lets you warm scorch-sensitive ingredients without direct flame. For freezing or refrigerator-setting: prefer a shallow, wide vessel (ice tray, shallow dish) over a tall deep one — better thermal contact with cold air and the shelf means faster, more even cooling/freezing, and a thinner layer sets uniformly without warm cores. For ganache or other set-by-chilling mixtures specifically, pouring from a deep bowl into a shallow dish before refrigerating shortens set time and produces a uniform fudge-like texture. Reserve mixing bowls and cups for off-heat prep or storage unless explicitly rated for the process.

entries.when_to_use: Selecting a heat level on the stove for a cooking task.
entries.strategy: Calibrate heat to the ingredient's scorch tolerance, not just speed. For narrow-window ingredients (butter, chocolate, sugar, milk, cream, eggs, cheese) default to low or medium-low and pull off slightly early so residual heat finishes the job — this avoids crispy edges, overcooked centers, seized chocolate, scalded milk, and grainy cheese (proteins seize over high heat). For robust liquid-based tasks with a clear endpoint (e.g., bringing water or vegetable soup to a rolling boil), HIGH heat is appropriate and fastest — water cannot scorch, only evaporate or boil over. For SEARING a dry, well-salted protein (steak, chops, thick fish fillets) on cast iron or carbon steel, HIGH heat is also correct and necessary — you need the surface ripping hot (lightly smoking, Leidenfrost beading) so the Maillard reaction starts on contact and the meat sears instead of steaming. Soups, stews, and milk-based liquids contain solids/proteins that CAN scorch on the bottom under sustained high heat, so stir occasionally even when using high heat for those. For absorption-cooked starches (rice, grains): start HIGH to reach a boil, then drop to LOW for the covered simmer so the bottom doesn't scorch as the water disappears. For milk and dairy-based liquids specifically: start medium-low (never high), step down to low as you approach the target temperature to avoid overshooting, and stir continuously.

entries.when_to_use: Heating liquid in a pot on the stovetop (e.g., bringing water or soup to a boil) where reaching temperature quickly matters.
entries.strategy: Place the pot on the burner BEFORE turning on the heat, then cover with a lid to trap heat and steam, which speeds the time to boil significantly. For soups, stews, or any liquid containing solids, leave the lid slightly ajar (small gap) so steam can vent without pressure buildup, and stir every couple of minutes with a wooden spoon to prevent the bottom from scorching. Stay near the stove and watch for the progression: small bubbles on the bottom, then rising bubbles, then a steady rolling boil with large continuous bubbles across the entire surface — that's the endpoint for a vigorous boil. Lift the lid only briefly to check or stir, since each lift releases trapped heat. Reduce heat promptly after reaching the rolling boil to prevent boil-over. Never leave a high burner unattended.

entries.when_to_use: Preheating a pan with butter for delicate items (eggs, fish, crepes) and needing to know when the temperature is right before adding the food.
entries.strategy: Use butter as a built-in thermometer. Add it to the warming pan and watch its behavior: if it just sits or slowly melts, the pan is too cool; if it foams lightly and spreads as a thin golden layer without browning, the pan is at the ideal medium-low to medium temperature — add the food now. If the butter browns quickly or smokes, the pan is too hot — pull it off the heat briefly to let it cool before continuing.

entries.when_to_use: Frying a sunny-side-up egg (or any egg where the yolk should stay intact and runny).
entries.strategy: Preheat a non-stick pan over medium-low with a teaspoon of butter until the butter foams without browning. Crack the egg on the pan's rim and slip the contents in CLOSE to the surface so the yolk doesn't break from the drop. Leave the egg completely undisturbed — no flipping, no nudging — for ~2–3 minutes until the whites are fully opaque and firm while the yolk stays domed and glossy. Turn off the heat and transfer immediately with a spatula so residual heat doesn't set the yolk.

entries.when_to_use: Choosing the right appliance for a temperature-change task in a kitchen with both a refrigerator and a freezer available.
entries.strategy: Match the appliance to the required end state AND to the container/ingredient's safety constraints. A refrigerator only chills (above 0°C) and will never solidify water or freeze foods; a freezer sustains sub-zero temperatures and is required for any phase change to solid ice or for long-term frozen storage. Don't substitute the fridge when the goal is freezing — the task will never complete. Conversely, don't reach for the freezer when the goal is merely 'cold to the touch,' 'chilled,' or 'set firm' (as for ganache, custards, mousses, or panna cotta) — for these the fridge's steady, gentle cold produces uniform texture, while the freezer hardens unevenly, can cause condensation/graininess, and for sealed carbonated beverages (soda, beer, sparkling water) or full liquid bottles can rupture the container or violently de-carbonate it. The fridge is the safe default for chilling and for setting dairy-rich emulsions; reserve the freezer for true freezing tasks or short, attended quick-chills of robust containers.

entries.when_to_use: Freezing water (or another liquid) into solid ice in a home freezer.
entries.strategy: Transfer the liquid from a tall cup into a shallow, wide vessel like an ice tray for better thermal contact and even freezing. Place the filled tray flat and level on a freezer shelf so it doesn't spill, then close the door promptly to maintain the cold environment. Allow ~2–4 hours of undisturbed freezing for a standard tray of water — check by looking for opaque, fully solid blocks rather than translucent or slushy centers. Avoid opening the freezer repeatedly during this window, since each opening releases cold air and slows the process.

entries.when_to_use: Deciding whether a kitchen task requires heating or chilling appliances at all, when the goal is purely physical combination (e.g., mixing a dough, batter, dressing, salad, or whisking eggs and milk).
entries.strategy: Read the goal literally — if it only asks to combine, whisk, or unify ingredients, no thermal step is needed and the stove/freezer should remain unused. Defaulting to heat or cold when the goal doesn't require a phase change wastes effort and can ruin the result (e.g., scrambling eggs you only meant to whisk). Reserve appliances for tasks whose endpoint explicitly involves a temperature change (melt, boil, fry, freeze, chill, set).

entries.when_to_use: Mixing dry and wet ingredients into a uniform dough or batter (e.g., flour + water + salt).
entries.strategy: Combine dry ingredients first in the mixing bowl and whisk them together so seasonings like salt disperse evenly and don't form pockets in the final product. Then add the liquid gradually in small increments while stirring with a wooden spoon, letting shaggy clumps form before adding more — this prevents a soupy mess and lets you feel when hydration is correct. Once no dry flour remains, turn the rough mass onto the counter and knead by folding and pressing for several minutes until the texture is smooth, elastic, and uniform in color. Return to a covered bowl for resting or use.

entries.when_to_use: Preparing a solid ingredient (chocolate bar, block of cheese, stick of butter) before applying heat to melt it.
entries.strategy: Unwrap fully and reduce the solid to small, even units before it goes into the heating vessel: break or chop chocolate, cube butter, and GRATE block cheese into shreds (grating is faster and more uniform than cubing for hard/semi-hard cheeses, and the high surface area lets cheese melt before its proteins seize). Uniform piece size means uniform melting — large chunks leave cold cores while small bits scorch, and stray wrappers can melt or burn. Smaller surface-area-per-volume also dramatically shortens melt time at low/gentle heat.

entries.when_to_use: Boiling or rapidly heating a soup, stew, or other liquid containing solid pieces (vegetables, meat, grains).
entries.strategy: Place the pot on the burner first, then cover with a lid set slightly ajar so steam can vent without pressure buildup. Turn the burner to High to drive the soup quickly toward a boil — water-based broths can take high heat. Stir every couple of minutes with a wooden spoon to lift any settled solids off the bottom; soups scorch from below because vegetables and starches stick where pure water wouldn't. Watch the surface progression: small bubbles, then steady steam under the lid, then a full rolling boil with large continuous bubbles churning the contents — that's a vigorous boil. Reduce heat to a simmer immediately after reaching it to prevent boil-over and overcooking.

entries.when_to_use: Cooking rice (or similar absorption-method grains like quinoa, farro) on the stovetop until tender.
entries.strategy: Use a ~2:1 water-to-rice ratio by volume in a pot with a tight-fitting lid. Combine rice and water in the cold pot, then place on the burner and bring to a rolling boil on HIGH uncovered. Once boiling, cover with the lid and drop the burner to LOW so the pot settles into a gentle simmer — at this point do NOT lift the lid, since escaping steam is what cooks the grain. Simmer covered for ~18 minutes for white rice (longer for brown/wild varieties), then turn the heat off and let the covered pot rest 5 minutes off-burner so residual steam finishes absorption and the grains firm up. Only then remove the lid and fluff with a fork to separate grains. Lifting the lid early releases the steam that cooks the rice and leaves crunchy centers or gummy texture.

entries.when_to_use: Chilling a warm sealed beverage (soda, beer, sparkling water, juice bottle) until it is cold to the touch, without freezing it.
entries.strategy: Use the refrigerator, NOT the freezer — carbonated and full liquid containers expand on freezing and can rupture or violently de-carbonate, and the goal is 'cold,' not 'frozen.' Stand the bottle UPRIGHT on an open shelf (middle shelf works well) so it doesn't roll and cold air can circulate around all sides for even cooling. Close the door promptly to retain cold air. Allow roughly 2–3 hours of undisturbed chilling for a room-temperature bottle to reach reliably cold-to-the-touch temperature; check by briefly touching the bottle — condensation beading on the exterior when it meets warm room air confirms it's properly chilled. Avoid repeated door openings, which release cold air and lengthen the wait. If a faster chill is needed and the container is robust, a short, ATTENDED freezer stint (15–20 minutes max with a timer) can work — but never leave a carbonated bottle in the freezer unattended or long enough to freeze.

entries.when_to_use: Whisking eggs (with or without milk/cream) into a uniform liquid base for omelettes, scrambles, custards, or French toast.
entries.strategy: Crack each egg one at a time on the bowl's rim — not against another egg or the bowl interior — so shell fragments stay out and a bad egg can be discarded without contaminating the rest. Add any liquid (about 1 tablespoon of milk or cream per egg lightens omelettes; skip for tighter scrambles) AFTER the eggs are in. Whisk briskly in a tight circular motion for ~30 seconds until yolks fully break and the mixture turns a uniform pale-yellow with light froth and no streaks of clear white or unmixed dairy. Finish by scraping the whisk along the bowl's sides and bottom to incorporate any clinging white, then give a few final stirs — unmixed pockets at the wall are the most common defect. Use immediately; eggs separate again on standing.

entries.when_to_use: Cracking raw eggs into a bowl for any recipe.
entries.strategy: Crack each egg one at a time on the bowl's rim (a flat, firm edge breaks the shell cleanly with fewer fragments than tapping on a flat surface or another egg). Open the shell directly over the bowl and drop the contents in, then discard the shell immediately so halves don't get confused with un-cracked eggs. Cracking one at a time — rather than all at once — lets you catch a bad egg or stray shell piece before it contaminates the whole batch; if a fragment falls in, scoop it out with a larger piece of shell (which attracts it) rather than fingers.

entries.when_to_use: Melting cheese (cheddar, gruyère, mozzarella, or similar) on the stovetop for a sauce, fondue, or queso — where the goal is a smooth, glossy melt without graininess or oil separation.
entries.strategy: Never apply dry direct heat to cheese — milk proteins seize and fat breaks out, leaving a grainy, greasy mess. Instead, build the cheese into a warm liquid medium: pour ~1 cup of milk (or cream, or a roux/béchamel base) into a saucepan, set the burner to LOW, and warm the milk until it is steaming but NOT boiling. Meanwhile grate the cheese into fine shreds (high surface area melts before proteins seize). Add the grated cheese in small handfuls, whisking continuously between additions so each handful disperses fully before the next goes in. Keep the heat low throughout — high heat will turn the sauce stringy or split it. Pull the saucepan off the burner the moment the sauce is uniformly smooth and glossy; residual heat will hold it pourable, and continued cooking risks breaking the emulsion.

entries.when_to_use: Heating milk (or cream, or any dairy-based liquid) on the stovetop to a precise sub-boil target like a scald or bare simmer (~180–185°F / 82–85°C) without scalding, skinning, or boiling over.
entries.strategy: Place the pot on the burner first and start at medium-low — never high — because milk scorches on the pot bottom and forms skin or boils over with little warning. Stir slowly and continuously with a wooden spoon, scraping the base, to prevent milk solids from settling and burning. Once gentle steam appears, clip a thermometer to the pot's side with the tip submerged but NOT touching the bottom (touching gives a misleadingly high reading). Watch the climb: around 170°F, when small bubbles start clinging to the sides, drop the burner to LOW to slow the rise and avoid overshooting into a rolling boil. The target bare simmer is ~180–185°F with tiny edge bubbles and a shimmering surface — not large bubbles. Hold briefly to confirm stability, then pull off the heat; residual heat will keep it warm for use. If a skin starts to form, whisk it back in or skim it off — don't let it thicken.

entries.when_to_use: Searing a thick cut of meat (steak, pork chop, lamb chop, thick fish fillet) on the stovetop with the goal of a deep Maillard crust and a controlled interior doneness like medium-rare.
entries.strategy: Three principles: dry surface, ripping-hot pan, hands off. (1) Prep the meat: pat it bone-dry with paper towels — surface moisture must boil off before browning can start, so a wet steak steams gray instead of searing. Salt generously and let the meat sit on the counter ~20 minutes to temper toward room temperature; cold meat into a hot pan drops the pan's temperature and cooks unevenly. (2) Preheat a heavy cast-iron or carbon-steel pan DRY on HIGH for ~4 minutes until it just starts to lightly smoke; the Leidenfrost test — a flicked water droplet skitters and beads instead of evaporating — confirms it's hot enough. Add a thin film of HIGH-smoke-point oil (avocado, refined grapeseed, refined safflower; NOT butter or olive) right before the meat goes in, not earlier, so the oil doesn't burn while you wait. (3) Lay the meat in away from you and DO NOT MOVE IT — fidgeting interrupts crust formation and the meat will stick. For a ~1-inch steak targeting medium-rare, sear ~2.5 minutes per side; the first side releases cleanly from the pan when the crust has set. Use an instant-read thermometer in the thickest part: pull at ~125–126°F (52°C) for medium-rare because carryover during rest adds ~5°F. Always rest the meat loosely tented on a board for ~5–7 minutes before slicing so muscle fibers relax and reabsorb juices — cutting immediately dumps juices onto the board and leaves the interior gray-wet. Slice against the grain for tenderness.

entries.when_to_use: Cooking any protein (or other dish) to a specific internal doneness using an instant-read thermometer.
entries.strategy: Account for carryover cooking by pulling the food off heat ~5°F (~3°C) BELOW the final target — residual heat trapped in the food keeps cooking it during rest. For steak: pull at 125–126°F for medium-rare (final ~130–131°F), 135°F for medium, 145°F for medium-well. Probe the thickest part of the food, keeping the thermometer tip away from bone or the pan surface (both read falsely high). Confirm with a second probe at a slightly different angle if the first reading is borderline. After pulling, rest the food loosely tented in a warm spot — tight foil traps too much steam and softens any crust; no cover at all loses temperature too fast. Resting time scales with thickness: ~5 minutes for a 1-inch steak, ~10 minutes for a thick roast.

entries.when_to_use: Setting a warm dairy-and-chocolate or cream-based mixture (ganache, truffle filling, custard, mousse, panna cotta) into a firm, scoopable or sliceable consistency by chilling.
entries.strategy: Use the REFRIGERATOR, not the freezer — steady fridge cold (~3–5°C) sets the fat-and-protein emulsion uniformly into a smooth, fudge-like texture, while the freezer's harsher cold hardens the outside before the core sets, can cause condensation that makes ganache grainy or splits the emulsion, and overshoots into rock-hard rather than scoopable. First transfer the warm mixture from a deep bowl into a SHALLOW dish, spreading it into a thin even layer (~2 cm); the increased surface area and reduced depth dramatically speed and even out the set. Place the dish flat on a level fridge shelf and close the door promptly. Leave undisturbed for ~2 hours for ganache (longer for thick custards/mousses); agitation during set can cause separation. Check by gently pressing the surface with a clean fingertip — properly set ganache is matte on top, firm but yields slightly under pressure (pliable, fudge-like), and holds its shape when scooped. If still fluid, return to the fridge in 30-minute increments; if rock-hard, you over-chilled — let it warm at room temperature a few minutes before scooping.

entries.when_to_use: Emulsifying two normally-immiscible liquids into a stable dressing or sauce — oil-and-vinegar vinaigrettes, lemon-and-oil dressings, and similar cold emulsions made by hand with a whisk.
entries.strategy: Three principles: vinegar-first, slow stream, constant whisking. (1) Pour the acidic/water-based phase (vinegar, citrus juice) into the bowl FIRST — typically a 1:3 acid-to-oil ratio for a balanced vinaigrette. Starting with the smaller-volume aqueous phase as the base lets oil disperse INTO it as tiny droplets, which is the geometry of a stable emulsion; reversing the order forces a less stable water-in-oil emulsion. (2) Whisk the vinegar briskly on its own for a few seconds first to agitate it, creating a frothy, energized base ready to accept oil. Any emulsifier (mustard, honey, egg yolk) goes in here and gets whisked in before oil. (3) Drizzle the oil in a THIN, STEADY STREAM while whisking continuously and vigorously — never dump the oil in at once, which leaves it as a separate floating layer that won't disperse. The mixture will visibly cloud and thicken as oil droplets break apart and disperse; keep whisking through the entire pour. Whisk an extra 10–15 seconds after the oil is in, then pause and check: a properly emulsified dressing is opaque, uniformly colored, coats the whisk, and holds together for at least several minutes without separating into distinct layers. Hand emulsions break over time — re-whisk just before serving if it has been sitting.

Swirl or nudge every ~20 seconds so the bottom stays in contact with the pan but doesn't scorch while the top is still solid. Pull off the heat when only a few small lumps remain and let residual heat finish melting — this avoids browning the milk solids.

entry.when_to_use: Searing a thick cut of meat (steak, pork chop, lamb chop, thick fish fillet) on the stovetop with the goal of a deep Maillard crust and a controlled interior doneness like medium-rare.

Always rest the meat loosely tented on a board for ~5–7 minutes before slicing so muscle fibers relax and reabsorb juices — cutting immediately dumps juices onto the board and leaves the interior gray-wet. entry.when_to_use: Setting a warm dairy-and-chocolate or cream-based mixture (ganache, truffle filling, custard, mousse, panna cotta) into a firm, scoopable or sliceable consistency by chilling.

entry.when_to_use: Emulsifying two normally-immiscible liquids into a stable dressing or sauce — oil-and-vinegar vinaigrettes, lemon-and-oil dressings, and similar cold emulsions made by hand with a whisk.

entries.when_to_use: When cooking white rice on a stovetop using the absorption method with a pot, lid, water, and a heat source.
entries.strategy: Use a 2:1 water-to-rice ratio: combine rice and water in the pot first, bring to a rolling boil on high uncovered, then cover and reduce to low for ~18 minutes without lifting the lid. After simmering, turn off the heat and let the covered pot rest ~5 minutes before fluffing with a fork. Avoid lifting the lid during simmer/rest to preserve steam and prevent uneven cooking or scorching.

entries.when_to_use: When chilling a carbonated beverage (soda, beer, sparkling water) from warm to cold-to-the-touch, and both a refrigerator and freezer are available.
entries.strategy: Choose the refrigerator over the freezer: place the bottle upright on a shelf with airflow, close the door, and wait ~2-3 hours. Avoid the freezer because freezing carbonated liquid expands and can rupture or burst the bottle, losing carbonation and creating a mess. Only consider the freezer for short, timed cooling (e.g., 15-20 min) if speed is critical and you can monitor it closely.

entries.when_to_use: When combining liquid ingredients (e.g., eggs and milk) into a uniform mixture using a whisk in a bowl, especially for omelette or batter bases.
entries.strategy: Sequence the work as: (1) add eggs first by cracking on the rim to avoid shells, (2) add a small ratio of milk (~1 tablespoon per egg), (3) whisk briskly in circular motion ~30 seconds until pale-yellow and slightly frothy with no streaks, and (4) finish by scraping the whisk along the bowl's sides and bottom to incorporate any clinging egg white before declaring uniformity. Verify success by visual check for absence of clear white streaks or unmixed milk pockets.

entries.when_to_use: When melting a block of hard cheese (e.g., cheddar) for a sauce on a stovetop, where direct dry heat would scorch the cheese and high heat would cause proteins to seize.
entries.strategy: Grate the cheese first to maximize surface area, warm a liquid medium (milk) in a saucepan over low heat, then add the shredded cheese in handfuls while whisking continuously until smooth and glossy; remove from heat promptly to preserve texture.

entries.when_to_use: When heating scorch-prone liquids like milk or cream to a bare simmer (~180-185°F / 82-85°C) on a stove without boiling over or burning the bottom.
entries.strategy: Start on medium-low heat and stir continuously with a flat-bottomed utensil, scraping the bottom to prevent solids from settling and scorching. Clip a thermometer to the pot wall with the tip submerged but not touching the bottom. Preemptively reduce heat to low around 170°F to avoid overshoot from residual heat. Confirm the target by visual cues (tiny edge bubbles, surface shimmer — not a rolling boil) alongside the thermometer reading, then pull off heat promptly once stable.

entries.when_to_use: When pan-searing a steak (or similar thick cut of meat) on a stovetop with cast iron, aiming for a Maillard crust at a specific internal doneness like medium-rare.
entries.strategy: Eliminate surface moisture before heat: pat dry, salt, and temper ~20 min. Preheat the dry cast-iron pan on high until it just smokes (verify with the Leidenfrost water-bead test), then add high-smoke-point oil only just before the steak. Lay the steak away from you, do not move it for ~2.5 min per side on a ~1-inch cut, flip once. Pull at ~125–126°F internal (instant-read in thickest part) since carryover adds ~5°F, then rest loosely tented ~7 min before slicing against the grain. Key invariants: dry surface, ripping-hot pan, oil added late, minimal fidgeting, pull below target temp, always rest.

entries.when_to_use: When solidifying a warm liquid fat-based emulsion (e.g., chocolate ganache) into a firm but pliable, scoopable consistency for truffles or similar confections, with both a refrigerator and freezer available.
entries.strategy: Pour the warm mixture into a shallow dish to maximize surface area for even cooling, then chill in the refrigerator (not the freezer, which causes uneven hardening and condensation/graininess) on a level shelf for roughly 2 hours undisturbed. Verify readiness by gently pressing the surface with a clean fingertip — it should feel firm yet yield slightly (fudge-like) before scooping.

entries.when_to_use: When combining oil and vinegar (or similar immiscible liquids) into a stable emulsion such as a vinaigrette, with access to a bowl and whisk.
entries.strategy: Start with the vinegar (the water-based phase) in the bowl and begin whisking it first to create an agitated base. Then drizzle the oil in a thin, steady stream while whisking continuously — roughly 3 parts oil to 1 part vinegar. Continue whisking for ~15 seconds after all oil is added, then pause to verify the mixture stays cloudy and uniform rather than separating. Avoid dumping oil in all at once, which prevents proper droplet dispersion and breaks the emulsion.

entries.when_to_use: When a wet mixture looks uniform in the center of the bowl after whisking or stirring
entries.strategy: Before declaring the task done, scrape the whisk or spatula along the sides and bottom of the bowl to incorporate any clinging unmixed material, then give a few final stirs — edge pockets are the most common source of residual non-uniformity.

entries.when_to_use: Cracking raw eggs into a mixing vessel for any recipe
entries.strategy: Crack eggs one at a time on the bowl's rim and drop the contents in while keeping shells separate, so a bad egg or shell fragment can be caught before contaminating the rest of the batch.

entries.when_to_use: Combining oil and vinegar (or any oil + aqueous liquid) into a stable vinaigrette or emulsion by hand
entries.strategy: Use roughly a 3:1 oil-to-vinegar ratio. Put the vinegar in the bowl first, begin whisking it briskly to agitate, then drizzle the oil in a thin, steady stream while whisking continuously rather than dumping it in. Continue whisking ~15 seconds after the oil is added and pause to verify the mixture stays uniform before considering the emulsion set.

entry.when_to_use: Melting butter or similar heat-sensitive dairy fat on a stovetop
entry.strategy: Use a saucepan (not a bowl) on low heat, unwrapped, and gently swirl/nudge every ~20 seconds so the fat stays in contact with the warm surface and the bottom does not scorch while the top is still solid.

verdicts.label: useful
verdicts.reasoning: The entry is correctly scoped to heat-melt for heat-sensitive dairy fat (butter), specifies low heat in a saucepan, and adds sensible technique details (swirling to prevent scorching). The when_to_use clause clearly restricts applicability to melting on a stovetop, so it would not misfire on boiling, cooling, mixing, or cooking tasks.

entry.when_to_use: Melting heat-sensitive ingredients like chocolate that scorch or seize above ~115°F
entry.strategy: Use a double-boiler: ~2 inches of water in a saucepan on low heat with a heat-safe bowl resting on the rim (not touching the water), so steam — not direct flame — provides gentle indirect heat.

entry.strategy: Grate the cheese first and melt it into a warm dairy medium (e.g., milk) over low heat with continuous whisking; never apply high direct heat to solid cheese, since proteins seize and turn grainy.

entry.strategy: Break the solid into small, evenly sized pieces before heating, and stir slowly throughout melting to distribute heat, eliminate hot spots, and achieve a uniform, lump-free consistency.

entry.when_to_use: Frying a sunny-side-up egg (or similar preparation requiring an intact runny yolk and fully-set whites)
entry.strategy: Use medium-low heat with butter as the cooking medium; preheat the pan, add butter and wait for light foam (not browning) to confirm temperature, then crack the egg close to the surface and leave undisturbed for 2-3 minutes until whites are opaque. Do not flip. Remove from heat promptly so residual heat does not overcook the yolk.

entry.when_to_use: Searing meat in a pan to develop a Maillard crust
entry.strategy: Eliminate surface moisture and cold before contact: pat dry, salt, and temper the meat while preheating a dry heavy pan (e.g., cast iron) on high until it just smokes or passes the Leidenfrost water-bead test. Add high-smoke-point oil only just before laying the meat down, then leave it undisturbed for ~2-3 minutes per side so the crust can set and release cleanly.
